title: The Multi-Task Challenge
concept: Training a model on multiple tasks simultaneously (coding, summaries, chat) requires balancing the loss contributions of each task in the training batch.

exercises.prompt: What does 'multi-task training' mean in machine learning?

options.text: Training one model on multiple different tasks at the same time.
Using ten different models for ten different users.
Deleting old skills to make room for a single new skill.

exercises.explain: Multi-task training teaches a single AI brain how to perform multiple different skills simultaneously.

cards.title: One Brain, Many Talents
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how ChatGPT can write a poem, debug your Python code, and translate French all in the same chatbox? It doesn't use three different brains—it learned them all at the exact same time! But teaching an AI to multitask without it getting confused is a delicate balancing act.

cards.title: What is Multi-Task Training?
cards.body: Multi-task training is when we teach a single model to do several different jobs at once, like summarizing text, writing code, and chatting. To make this work, we combine the mistakes (loss) from all tasks into a single score, but we must balance them so no single task dominates the training.

cards.title: The Multi-Sport Athlete
cards.body: Imagine a decathlon athlete training for running, high jump, and shot put. If they only focus on high jump because it's fun, they will fail the other events. To win the whole decathlon, they must split their training time and energy carefully among all three sports so they improve at everything at once.

cards.title: Balancing the Scales
cards.body: If we train an AI on coding and chatting, the coding errors might naturally be 10 times larger than the chat errors. To prevent the AI from ignoring chat, we multiply the chat error by a balancing weight (like 10) to make both tasks equally important in the batch.

cards.title: The Equal Data Myth
cards.body: It seems logical that if you feed a model equal amounts of data for each task, it will learn them equally. But this is a myth! Some tasks are naturally harder or generate much larger error values. We must actively balance the mathematical 'loss' (error score), not just the data size.

cards.title: Balanced vs. Unbalanced Training
cards.body: Visualizing how unbalanced training leads to a one-sided model, while balanced training creates a well-rounded AI assistant.

left.text: Both tasks get equal attention. The model learns to code and chat successfully.

right.text: Coding errors dominate. The model becomes great at coding but terrible at chatting.

exercises.prompt: What happens if one task's error (loss) is much larger than the others and we don't balance them?

options.text: The model will instantly become perfect at all tasks.
The model will focus only on the high-loss task and ignore the others.
The model will stop training and crash immediately.
The high-loss task will be automatically deleted from the brain.

exercises.explain: If one task has a massive loss, the model will spend all its energy correcting that task's mistakes, completely ignoring the other tasks.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you have the exact same amount of training data for two tasks, you do not need to balance their losses.
exercises.explain: Even with identical amounts of data, different tasks have different difficulties and error ranges, requiring loss balancing.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the training rule.
exercises.sentence: To train an AI on multiple tasks at once, we must balance the contribution of each task's ___ in the batch.
exercises.explain: We must balance the mathematical loss (error score) of each task so that the model doesn't focus entirely on the task with the largest errors.

exercises.prompt: Which formula represents a balanced multi-task loss calculation?

options.text: Total Loss = Coding Loss + Chatting Loss (without weights)
Total Loss = (0.2 * Coding Loss) + (0.8 * Chatting Loss)
Total Loss = Coding Loss (Chatting is ignored)

exercises.explain: Option B shows a balanced approach where both task losses are given appropriate weights (0.2 and 0.8) to prevent one from dominating. Option A ignores balancing, and Option C only trains on one task.

exercises.prompt: You are building a super-AI that writes code and chats with users. However, the coding errors are huge and completely drown out the chat training. How do you balance the training?

options.text: Add multiplier weights to scale the loss of each task before combining them.
Train the model on only one task per week and hope it remembers the others.
Keep all task losses exactly equal and ignore how fast the model learns them.
Delete the harder tasks so the model only has to learn the easiest one.

exercises.explain: A single learning rate is not enough because tasks like coding and chatting can have very different difficulty levels and error scales. Without scaling factors, the hardest task can completely drown out the easier ones.
